title: Strings In Depth
subtitle: Learn how to create, measure, and access individual characters in strings
learning_objectives: Create strings using single quotes, double quotes, and triple quotes
Write multiline strings using triple quotes and the \n escape character
Measure the length of a string using the len() function
Access individual characters in a string using positive and negative indexing

sections.heading: Creating Strings
sections.content:
A string is a sequence of characters wrapped in quotes. Python gives you three ways to create strings.

Use single quotes or double quotes for short, one-line strings — they work exactly the same. The only reason to pick one over the other is when your text itself contains a quote character.

Use triple quotes (""" or ''') when you want a string that spans multiple lines. Everything between the opening and closing triple quotes is included in the string, including line breaks.
sections.code_example:
# Single and double quotes — identical behavior
greeting = 'Hello, world!'
also_greeting = "Hello, world!"

# Use double quotes when text has an apostrophe
message = "It's a great day!"

# Use single quotes when text has double quotes
quote = 'She said "wow"'

# Triple quotes for multiline strings
poem = """Roses are red,
Violets are blue,
Python is great,
And so are you!"""

print(greeting)
print(message)
print(poem)
sections.code_output:
Hello, world!
It's a great day!
Roses are red,
Violets are blue,
Python is great,
And so are you!

sections.heading: String Length and the \n Escape Character
sections.content:
The len() function tells you how many characters are in a string. Every character counts — letters, spaces, punctuation, even invisible ones.

The newline escape character is a special two-character sequence that Python treats as a single line-break character. When Python prints , it moves to the next line. You can use inside regular (non-triple-quoted) strings to create multiline output.

Because counts as one character, len("Hi Bye") returns 6, not 7.
sections.code_example:
word = "Python"
print(len(word))       # 6 characters

sentence = "Hi there!"
print(len(sentence))   # 9 characters (space counts!)

# \n inside a regular string creates a line break
two_lines = "Line one\nLine two"
print(two_lines)

# \n counts as one character
print(len(two_lines))  # 'Line one' (8) + '\n' (1) + 'Line two' (8) = 17

sections.heading: Indexing: Accessing Individual Characters
sections.content:
You can pick out any single character from a string using indexing. Write the variable name followed by a number inside square brackets: word[0].

Positive indexes start at 0 from the left. The first character is always at index 0, the second at 1, and so on.

Negative indexes count from the right. Index -1 is always the last character, -2 is the second-to-last, and so on. Negative indexing is handy when you want the end of a string without knowing its exact length.

If you try to use an index that is out of range (e.g. index 10 on a 6-character string), Python raises an IndexError.
sections.code_example:
name = "Python"
#        P  y  t  h  o  n
# index: 0  1  2  3  4  5
# neg:  -6 -5 -4 -3 -2 -1

print(name[0])   # First character
print(name[1])   # Second character
print(name[5])   # Last character (positive index)
print(name[-1])  # Last character (negative index)
print(name[-2])  # Second-to-last character
print(name[-6])  # Same as name[0]
sections.code_output:
P
y
n
n
o
P

exercises.title: Create and Measure a String
exercises.prompt: Create a variable called `city` and assign it the string `"New York"`. Then print the length of the string using `len()`.
exercises.starter_code:
# Create a variable called city and assign the string "New York" to it

# Print its length
hints: Assign the string like this: city = "New York"
Pass the variable to len() and then to print(): print(len(city))

exercises.title: Multiline String with \n
exercises.prompt: Create a variable called `address` that contains three lines using `\n`: `"123 Main St"`, `"Springfield"`, and `"USA"`. Print the variable.
exercises.starter_code:
# Create a variable called address using \n to separate "123 Main St", "Springfield", and "USA"

# Print the address
hints:
Put 
 between each line inside the string: "line1
line2
line3"
Make sure all three parts are in one string separated by \n
Common mistake: /n does nothing — it must be a backslash \n, not a forward slash /n
Also make sure address is one single string, not a tuple: address = "line1\nline2\nline3"

tests.message: Springfield appears on its own line (use \n, not /n)
USA appears on its own line (use \n, not /n)

exercises.title: Character Indexing
exercises.prompt: Given the string `word = "banana"`, print the first character, the last character using a negative index, and the third character (index 2).
exercises.starter_code:
word = "banana"

# Print the first character

# Print the last character (use a negative index)

# Print the third character (index 2)
hints: The first character is at index 0: word[0]
The last character uses index -1: word[-1]
The third character is at index 2: word[2]

quiz.question: What does `len("hello\n")` return?
quiz.explanation: The string contains 5 letters ('h','e','l','l','o') plus the newline character '\n', which counts as a single character. So the total length is 6.

quiz.question: Given `s = "Python"`, what does `s[-2]` return?
quiz.explanation: Negative indexing counts from the right. Index -1 is 'n', so index -2 is the character before it, which is 'o'.

completion_message: Great work! You now know how to create strings with different quote styles, use \n for line breaks, measure strings with len(), and access any character by its index. These skills are the foundation for everything else you will do with text in Python.
